preamble.summary: Absalom holds court in Jerusalem as a rival king and summons two counselors to advise his pursuit of David. Ahithophel proposes a swift night strike with twelve thousand men to kill David alone and bring the people back peacefully. Hushai, secretly loyal to David, counters with a grandiose plan to gather all Israel from Dan to Beersheba and overwhelm David with massive force — a plan designed to buy David time. Absalom and Israel's elders choose Hushai's counsel, and the narrator pauses to explain why: the LORD had ordained the defeat of Ahithophel's good advice so that disaster would fall on Absalom. Hushai sends word through the priests Zadok and Abiathar, whose sons Jonathan and Ahimaaz relay the warning to David despite a close pursuit that forces them to hide in a well at Bahurim. David crosses the Jordan to safety. Ahithophel, seeing his counsel rejected and the rebellion's doom sealed, rides home, sets his affairs in order, and hangs himself. The chapter closes with David arriving at Mahanaim, where Shobi, Makir, and Barzillai provide lavish supplies for his exhausted company.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter is one of the great political thrillers of the Hebrew Bible. The contest between Ahithophel and Hushai is not merely a debate between two advisors — it is a covert intelligence operation embedded in a theological narrative. Ahithophel's plan is, by the narrator's own admission, 'good counsel' (etsah tovah, v. 14): militarily sound, surgically precise, designed to end the war in a single night with minimal bloodshed. Hushai's plan is deliberately bad strategy — it calls for delay, mass mobilization, and an absurdly large army, all of which give David exactly what he needs: time. The narrator interrupts the political drama to deliver the chapter's theological verdict in verse 14: 'The LORD had ordained to frustrate the good counsel of Ahithophel, in order to bring disaster upon Absalom.' This is one of the Bible's clearest statements of divine sovereignty operating through human political decisions. Ahithophel's suicide in verse 23 is narrated with startling economy — three clauses covering his journey home, the settling of his estate, and his death by hanging. No moral commentary is offered. He is the only suicide in the Hebrew Bible whose death is described with the clinical detail that he 'set his house in order' (tsivvah el beito), the same phrase used for a patriarch preparing for death with dignity. The narrator neither condemns nor pities him; the facts speak for themselves.
preamble.friction: The primary interpretive tension lies in the theological claim of verse 14. If the LORD ordained the defeat of Ahithophel's counsel, what is the moral status of the human actors? Hushai is lying and manipulating — is his deception sanctioned by divine purpose? The text does not moralize about Hushai's methods; it simply reports that his counter-counsel served the LORD's purpose. A second friction concerns the spy network of verses 15-22. The priests Zadok and Abiathar, the female servant, the unnamed woman of Bahurim who hides the messengers — these are all active participants in espionage against the reigning government in Jerusalem. The narrative celebrates their cunning without apology. A third point of friction: Ahithophel's counsel is called 'good' by the narrator, meaning that from a purely strategic standpoint, Absalom would have won had he followed it. The rebellion fails not because it lacked good strategy but because God intervened through the inferior plan. This is uncomfortable for readers who want to see rebellion as inherently doomed by its own incompetence.
preamble.connections: Ahithophel's role connects backward to 2 Samuel 15:12, where his defection from David is first reported, and to 2 Samuel 15:31, where David prays, 'O LORD, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness' — this chapter is the direct answer to that prayer. Hushai's infiltration was set up in 2 Samuel 15:32-37, making chapters 15-17 a continuous narrative arc. The woman of Bahurim who hides the messengers (v. 19) recalls Rahab hiding the spies in Joshua 2 — both are women who protect fugitives by deceiving the authorities with false directions. Ahithophel's suicide anticipates the only other recorded hanging in the Hebrew Bible tradition that later readers would connect to betrayal — Judas in Matthew 27:5, and early Christian interpreters drew the parallel explicitly. The provisions brought to David at Mahanaim (vv. 27-29) echo the provisions Abigail brought to David in 1 Samuel 25, and Barzillai's generosity here will be remembered on David's deathbed (1 Kings 2:7). Mahanaim itself connects to Jacob's encounter with angels in Genesis 32:2 — the place where the fugitive patriarch found divine protection is now the refuge of the fugitive king.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֲחִיתֹ֙פֶל֙ אֶל־אַבְשָׁל֔וֹם אֶבְחֲרָ֣ה נָּ֗א שְׁנֵים־עָשָׂ֥ר אֶ֛לֶף אִ֖ישׁ וְאָק֣וּמָה וְאֶרְדְּפָ֗ה אַחֲרֵ֥י דָוִ֛ד הַלָּֽיְלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Moreover Ahithophel said unto Absalom, Let me now choose out twelve thousand men, and I will arise and pursue after David this night:
verses.rendering: Then Ahithophel said to Absalom, "Let me select twelve thousand men, and I will set out tonight in pursuit of David.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ahithophel opens with a first-person proposal of decisive action: evcharah na ('let me select') uses the voluntative form, asserting personal initiative. His plan is precise — twelve thousand men, not a general mobilization. The number is large enough to overwhelm David's reduced force but small enough to move fast. The critical word is hallaylah ('tonight'): Ahithophel understands that speed is everything. David is fleeing, exhausted, disorganized, and every hour of delay allows him to regroup, cross the Jordan, and establish a defensible position. Ahithophel's military instinct is to strike before the fugitive can recover.
translator_notes: The verb bachar ('to choose, to select') implies careful culling of the best troops, not a mass levy. Ahithophel envisions an elite strike force, not a national army. The phrase ve-aqomah ve-erdephah ('and I will arise and I will pursue') uses two cohortatives in sequence, expressing urgent personal determination — Ahithophel is volunteering to lead the mission himself, not delegating it.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָב֣וֹא עָלָ֗יו וְה֤וּא יָגֵ֙עַ֙ וּרְפֵ֣ה יָדַ֔יִם וְהַֽחֲרַדְתִּ֣י אֹת֔וֹ וְנָ֖ס כָּל־הָעָ֣ם אֲשֶׁר־אִתּ֑וֹ וְהִכֵּיתִ֥י אֶת־הַמֶּ֖לֶךְ לְבַדּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And I will come upon him while he is weary and weak handed, and will make him afraid: and all the people that are with him shall flee; and I will smite the king only:
verses.rendering: I will come upon him while he is exhausted and his hands are slack. I will throw him into panic, and all the people with him will scatter. Then I will strike down the king alone.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ahithophel's plan targets David's physical and psychological vulnerability: yagea ('exhausted') and repheh yadayim ('slack of hands,' meaning his grip on both weapon and leadership has weakened). The verb hacharadti ('I will cause him to tremble') is a Hiphil of charad, meaning to induce terror — Ahithophel plans a shock attack that will shatter morale before blades are drawn. The tactical genius lies in the final clause: vehikkeiti et hammelekh levaddo ('I will strike down the king — him alone'). This is a decapitation strike. Ahithophel has no interest in slaughtering David's followers; he wants to kill one man and bring everyone else home. It is the most humane military option available, which makes the narrator's description of it as 'good counsel' in verse 14 all the more pointed.
translator_notes: The phrase repheh yadayim ('slack of hands') is a Hebrew idiom for demoralization and physical depletion. Hands that cannot grip a weapon cannot fight. The same idiom appears in Isaiah 35:3 and Jeremiah 38:4. Ahithophel reads David's condition with clinical precision.

The word levaddo ('him alone') is the pivot of the entire plan. Ahithophel is proposing targeted assassination, not battle. By isolating David as the sole target, he minimizes casualties on both sides. This is not bloodlust — it is cold strategic calculation.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָשִׁ֤יבָה כָל־הָעָם֙ אֵלֶ֔יךָ כְּשׁ֣וּב הַכֹּ֔ל הָאִ֖ישׁ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אַתָּ֣ה מְבַקֵּ֑שׁ כָּל־הָעָ֖ם יִהְיֶ֥ה שָׁלֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: And I will bring back all the people unto thee: the man whom thou seekest is as if all returned: so all the people shall be in peace.
verses.rendering: Then I will bring all the people back to you. When the one man you are seeking is dead, all the people will return in peace."
translator_notes: The phrase keshov hakkol ('when the whole returns') is textually difficult. Some read it as 'the return of the whole depends on [the death of] the man you seek.' The Septuagint reads it differently, suggesting the Hebrew may be slightly corrupt. The essential meaning is clear: kill David, and the civil war ends instantly.

Ahithophel's use of shalom at the conclusion is a rhetorical masterstroke. He frames regicide as peacemaking. The audience — Absalom and the elders — hears a plan that combines surgical precision with national healing. It is nearly irresistible.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּישַׁ֥ר הַדָּבָ֖ר בְּעֵינֵ֣י אַבְשָׁל֑וֹם וּבְעֵינֵ֖י כָּל־זִקְנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And the saying pleased Absalom well, and all the elders of Israel.
verses.rendering: The proposal seemed right to Absalom and to all the elders of Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb vayyishar ('it was straight, it was right') comes from the root yashar, meaning to be upright, direct, and pleasing. Ahithophel's counsel strikes everyone as correct — not merely appealing but logically sound. The phrase be-einei Avshalom uve-einei khol ziqnei Yisra'el ('in the eyes of Absalom and in the eyes of all the elders of Israel') indicates unanimous approval. The 'elders of Israel' (ziqnei Yisra'el) are the tribal heads who have thrown in their lot with Absalom's rebellion. Their approval means this is not a private conversation but a formal war council, and Ahithophel has just won a unanimous vote. What follows — the consultation of Hushai — is therefore not a natural next step but an interruption of what should have been immediate action.
translator_notes: The narrator's note that the plan 'was right in the eyes of' both Absalom and the elders underscores that Ahithophel's counsel was not merely clever but genuinely persuasive to experienced leaders. The elders are not fools; they recognize good strategy. This makes verse 14's theological override all the more dramatic.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אַבְשָׁל֔וֹם קִרְא֣וּ נָ֔א גַּ֖ם לְחוּשַׁ֣י הָאַרְכִּ֑י וְנִשְׁמְעָ֥ה מַה־בְּפִ֖יו גַּם־הֽוּא׃
verses.text_kjv: Then said Absalom, Call now Hushai the Archite also, and let us hear likewise what he saith.
verses.rendering: But Absalom said, "Summon Hushai the Arkite as well. Let us hear what he has to say too."
verses.expanded_rendering: Absalom's decision to consult Hushai is the hinge on which the entire chapter turns. The word gam ('also, as well') appears twice in this verse — gam le-Chushai ('also Hushai') and gam hu ('him too') — as if Absalom is treating this as a formality, a second opinion to confirm what has already been decided. But this seemingly innocuous act of due diligence will destroy Ahithophel's plan, buy David enough time to cross the Jordan, and ultimately doom Absalom's rebellion. The narrator does not yet explain why Absalom wants a second opinion when the first was unanimously approved. Perhaps Absalom is cautious; perhaps he wants to test his new counselor; perhaps the LORD is already at work in his mind, moving him toward the decision that will be his undoing.
translator_notes: Hushai is identified as ha-Arki ('the Arkite'), indicating his clan or geographic origin from the town of Erech or Ataroth near the Benjamin-Ephraim border. This ethnic marker distinguishes him from the court insiders and may explain why Absalom sees him as an independent voice worth consulting. In fact, Hushai was planted by David (2 Samuel 15:32-37) for precisely this moment.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּבֹ֣א חוּשַׁי֮ אֶל־אַבְשָׁלוֹם֒ וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אַבְשָׁל֤וֹם אֵלָיו֙ כַּדָּבָ֣ר הַזֶּ֔ה דִּבֶּ֖ר אֲחִיתֹ֑פֶל הֲנַעֲשֶׂ֥ה אֶת־דְּבָר֛וֹ אִם־אַ֖יִן אַתָּ֥ה דַבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And when Hushai was come to Absalom, Absalom spake unto him, saying, Ahithophel hath spoken after this manner: shall we do after his saying? if not; speak thou.
verses.rendering: When Hushai came before Absalom, Absalom told him, "Ahithophel has proposed such-and-such. Should we follow his plan? If not, speak up."
translator_notes: The syntax im ayin attah dabber ('if not, you speak') gives Hushai explicit permission to disagree. Absalom is not looking for a yes-man — or at least, he does not think he is. The irony is that by inviting dissent, Absalom opens the door to the very deception that will destroy him.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר חוּשַׁ֖י אֶל־אַבְשָׁל֑וֹם לֹא־טוֹבָ֧ה הָעֵצָ֛ה אֲשֶׁר־יָעַ֥ץ אֲחִיתֹ֖פֶל בַּפַּ֥עַם הַזֹּֽאת׃
verses.text_kjv: And Hushai said unto Absalom, The counsel that Ahithophel hath given is not good at this time.
verses.rendering: Hushai said to Absalom, "The counsel Ahithophel has given is not sound — this time."
translator_notes: The phrase bappa'am hazzot ('at this time, on this occasion') is Hushai's most delicate rhetorical move. By limiting his objection to this single instance, he avoids the appearance of rivalry and positions himself as a situational corrective rather than a permanent replacement. It is the language of a loyal advisor, not a competitor.

key_terms.semantic_range: counsel, advice, strategic plan, deliberative wisdom, purpose, design
key_terms.note: Etsah is the word for considered, deliberative advice — not casual suggestion but formal strategic counsel given in a context of authority. It derives from the root ya'ats ('to counsel, to advise') and carries the weight of professional expertise. In this chapter it functions almost as a character in its own right: Ahithophel's etsah versus Hushai's etsah, with the LORD adjudicating between them. The same root appears in Isaiah 9:6 where the messianic king is called yo'ets ('counselor'), and in Isaiah 11:2 where the Spirit of the LORD includes ruach etsah ('the spirit of counsel'). Counsel in the Hebrew Bible is not mere opinion — it is the application of wisdom to decision-making, and its acceptance or rejection determines the fate of kingdoms.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר חוּשַׁ֗י אַתָּ֤ה יָדַ֙עְתָּ֙ אֶת־אָבִ֣יךָ וְאֶת־אֲנָשָׁ֔יו כִּ֥י גִבֹּרִ֛ים הֵ֖מָּה וּמָרֵ֣י נֶ֑פֶשׁ הֵ֗מָּה כְּדֹ֛ב שַׁכּ֥וּל בַּשָּׂדֶ֖ה וְאָבִ֙יךָ֙ אִ֣ישׁ מִלְחָמָ֔ה וְלֹ֥א יָלִ֖ין אֶת־הָעָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Hushai said, Thou knowest thy father and his men, that they be mighty men, and they be chafed in their minds, as a bear robbed of her whelps in the field: and thy father is a man of war, and will not lodge with the people.
verses.rendering: Hushai continued, "You know your father and his men — they are warriors, and they are fierce, like a bear robbed of her cubs in the open field. Besides, your father is a man of war. He will not spend the night with the main body of troops.
translator_notes: The phrase marei nephesh ('bitter of soul') describes men pushed past the point of fear into desperation. The same phrase is used in Judges 18:25 for men who will kill without hesitation because they have nothing left to lose. Hushai is telling Absalom: these men are not demoralized; they are dangerous precisely because they are cornered.

The bear simile (dov shakkul) is among the most vivid animal comparisons in the Hebrew Bible. The adjective shakkul ('bereaved') specifically means robbed of offspring — the bear's rage is not predatory but maternal, protective, and beyond reason. Hushai is not describing David's military skill but his emotional state: a father fighting for his life and his children.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֨ה עַתָּ֤ה הֽוּא־נֶחְבָּא֙ בְּאַחַ֣ת הַפְּחָתִ֔ים א֖וֹ בְּאַחַ֣ד הַמְּקוֹמֹ֑ת וְהָיָ֗ה כִּנְפֹ֤ל בָּהֶם֙ בַּתְּחִלָּ֔ה וְשָׁמַע֙ הַשֹּׁמֵ֔עַ וְאָמַ֗ר הָֽיְתָה֙ מַגֵּפָ֔ה בָּעָ֕ם אֲשֶׁ֖ר אַחֲרֵ֥י אַבְשָׁלֹֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, he is hid now in some pit, or in some other place: and it will come to pass, when some of them be overthrown at the first, that whosoever heareth it shall say, There is a slaughter among the people that follow Absalom.
verses.rendering: Right now he is hiding in some ravine or some other place. And when some of your men fall at the first clash, anyone who hears the news will say, 'There has been a slaughter among the troops following Absalom.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Hushai shifts from David's character to David's tactics: hinneh attah hu nechba ('look — right now he is hiding'). The verb chava ('to hide') in the Niphal pictures David concealed and unreachable. The word pechattim ('pits, ravines, hollows') evokes the Judean wilderness terrain where David spent years as a fugitive — he knows every cave and wadi. Hushai then constructs a psychological scenario: if Ahithophel's strike force encounters David's men in the dark and suffers even initial casualties (kinphol bahem battechillah, 'when some fall among them at the start'), the rumor mill will instantly transform a skirmish into a catastrophe. The phrase shama ha-shomea ('the one who hears will hear') is a Hebrew idiom for the unstoppable spread of news. Hushai is weaponizing the fear of rumor: even a minor setback will be reported as a disaster, and the narrative of Absalom's invincibility — essential to holding his fragile coalition together — will shatter overnight.
translator_notes: Hushai's argument exploits the information asymmetry of ancient warfare. Without reliable communication, the first report from the front determines public perception. If the first report says 'our men are dying,' the coalition collapses before the facts can be verified. This is sophisticated psychological warfare theory, dressed up as common-sense caution.

verses.text_hebrew: וְה֣וּא גַם־בֶּן־חַ֗יִל אֲשֶׁ֤ר לִבּוֹ֙ כְּלֵ֣ב הָֽאַרְיֵ֔ה הִמֵּ֖ס יִמָּ֑ס כִּֽי־יֹדֵ֤עַ כָּל־יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ כִּי־גִבּ֣וֹר אָבִ֔יךָ וּבְנֵי־חַ֖יִל אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And he also that is valiant, whose heart is as the heart of a lion, shall utterly melt: for all Israel knoweth that thy father is a mighty man, and they which be with him are valiant men.
verses.rendering: Then even the bravest soldier, one whose heart is like a lion's, will completely lose his nerve — because all Israel knows that your father is a warrior and the men with him are battle-hardened fighters."
verses.expanded_rendering: Hushai drives his fear campaign to its climax: even a ben chayil ('son of valor,' meaning a proven warrior) whose heart is kelev ha-aryeh ('like the heart of a lion') will himmes yimmas ('utterly melt'). The infinitive absolute himmes before the finite yimmas intensifies the verb to its maximum: not just wavering but total psychological collapse. Hushai then delivers the line that makes his argument irrefutable: ki yodea kol Yisra'el ki gibbor avikha ('because all Israel knows that your father is a warrior'). He invokes common knowledge — something Absalom cannot dispute. David's reputation as a gibbor is not propaganda but established fact, known from Dan to Beersheba. The implication is devastating: anyone you send against David in the dark will know who they are fighting, and that knowledge will paralyze them before the first blow falls.
translator_notes: The lion simile (lev ha-aryeh) applied to the bravest soldier who will still melt in fear is Hushai's rhetorical counterpoint to the bear simile he applied to David. David is the bear — irrational, unstoppable maternal rage. The best of Absalom's men is the lion — brave, but rational enough to feel fear. Hushai has arranged the animal kingdom so that David wins the comparison.

The verb masas ('to melt') in the Niphal with the infinitive absolute is the language of total demoralization. The same construction appears in Joshua 2:11 and 7:5 for the melting of hearts before an overwhelming enemy. Hushai borrows the language of Canaanite terror before Israel and turns it on Israel itself.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י יָעַ֗צְתִּי הֵאָסֹ֣ף יֵאָסֵף֩ עָלֶ֨יךָ כָל־יִשְׂרָאֵ֜ל מִדָּ֣ן וְעַד־בְּאֵ֥ר שֶׁ֙בַע֙ כַּח֣וֹל אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־הַיָּ֔ם לָרֹ֑ב וּפָנֶ֥יךָ הֹלְכִ֖ים בַּקְרָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore I counsel that all Israel be generally gathered unto thee, from Dan even to Beersheba, as the sand that is by the sea for multitude; and that thou go to battle in thine own person.
verses.rendering: Here is what I advise: muster all Israel to your side, from Dan to Beersheba — as countless as the sand on the seashore — and you yourself march into battle at their head.
translator_notes: The phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba' is the standard Hebrew designation for the full extent of Israel, from the northernmost settlement to the southernmost. A general mobilization from this range would take weeks to accomplish — exactly the delay Hushai needs to give David.

The sand simile (kachol asher al hayyam) is Abrahamic covenant language. Hushai is implicitly telling Absalom: you will command forces like those God promised to Abraham. The flattery is thick and deliberate, targeting Absalom's known vanity (2 Samuel 14:25-26).

verses.text_hebrew: וּבָ֣אנוּ אֵלָ֗יו בְּאַחַ֤ד הַמְּקוֹמוֹת֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר נִמְצָ֣א שָׁ֔ם וְנַ֣חְנוּ עָלָ֔יו כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר יִפֹּ֥ל הַטַּ֛ל עַל־הָאֲדָמָ֖ה וְלֹא־נוֹתַ֣ר בּ֑וֹ וּבְכָל־הָאֲנָשִׁ֥ים אֲשֶׁר־אִתּ֖וֹ גַּם־אֶחָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: So shall we come upon him in some place where he shall be found, and we will light upon him as the dew falleth on the ground: and of him and of all the men that are with him there shall not be left so much as one.
verses.rendering: Then we will descend on him wherever he is found, and we will settle on him the way dew falls on the ground — and not one will be left alive, neither he nor any of the men with him.
translator_notes: The dew image (tal) is usually positive in the Hebrew Bible — a symbol of blessing and refreshment (Genesis 27:28, Hosea 14:5, Micah 5:7). Hushai inverts the image, turning blessing-language into threat-language. This rhetorical inversion mirrors his entire performance: everything sounds strong but is actually designed to weaken Absalom's position.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאִם־אֶל־עִיר֙ יֵאָסֵ֔ף וְהִשִּׂ֧יאוּ כָֽל־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל אֶל־הָעִ֥יר הַהִ֖יא חֲבָלִ֑ים וְסָחַ֣בְנוּ אֹת֔וֹ עַד־הַנַּ֕חַל עַ֛ד אֲשֶֽׁר־לֹא־נִמְצָ֥א שָׁ֖ם גַּם־צְרֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: Moreover, if he be gotten into a city, then shall all Israel bring ropes to that city, and we will draw it into the river, until there be not one small stone found there.
verses.rendering: And if he retreats into a city, then all Israel will bring ropes to that city, and we will drag it stone by stone into the ravine until not even a pebble remains there."
verses.expanded_rendering: Hushai reaches peak rhetorical absurdity: if David takes refuge in a fortified city, all Israel will bring chavalim ('ropes') and drag the entire city into the nachal ('wadi, ravine'). The image is physically impossible and deliberately fantastical — Hushai is painting a picture of such overwhelming power that Absalom's ego cannot resist it. The phrase ad asher lo nimtsa sham gam tsror ('until not even a pebble is found there') takes the destruction down to the smallest unit, a tsror ('pebble, small stone'). The hyperbole is theatrical: Hushai is performing confidence, not offering strategy. He wants Absalom to feel invincible so that Absalom will choose the slow, massive, ego-gratifying plan over Ahithophel's fast, efficient, humble one. And it works.
translator_notes: The word chavalim ('ropes') used for dragging a city into a wadi is unique in military contexts. Some commentators see it as metaphorical siege language; others take it as deliberate comic exaggeration. Either way, the proposal is impractical on its face — but Hushai delivers it with such confidence that the council accepts the spirit of overwhelming force without questioning the logistics.

The word tsror ('pebble') is the same word used in 1 Samuel 25:29 for the 'bundle of the living' (tsror ha-chayyim). Here it is reduced to its most literal sense: a small stone. The juxtaposition is ironic — David's life is bound in the tsror of the living by God's promise, and here Hushai promises to destroy every tsror of the city. One promise is real; the other is theater.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אַבְשָׁלוֹם֙ וְכָל־אִ֣ישׁ יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל טוֹבַ֗ת עֲצַ֛ת חוּשַׁ֥י הָאַרְכִּ֖י מֵעֲצַ֣ת אֲחִיתֹ֑פֶל וַיהוָ֣ה צִוָּ֗ה לְהָפֵ֞ר אֶת־עֲצַ֤ת אֲחִיתֹ֙פֶל֙ הַטּוֹבָ֔ה לְבַעֲב֗וּר הָבִ֧יא יְהוָ֛ה אֶל־אַבְשָׁל֖וֹם אֶת־הָרָעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Absalom and all the men of Israel said, The counsel of Hushai the Archite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel. For the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom.
verses.rendering: Absalom and all the men of Israel declared, "The counsel of Hushai the Arkite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel." For the LORD had determined to frustrate the good counsel of Ahithophel, so that the LORD would bring disaster upon Absalom.
translator_notes: This is one of the Hebrew Bible's most explicit statements of divine sovereignty operating through secondary human causes. The LORD does not appear in a vision, send a prophet, or perform a miracle. He works through a political debate, a flattering speech, and a young king's vanity. The doctrine of providence here is not abstract theology but narrative demonstration: God accomplishes his will through the free decisions of human actors.

The adjective tovah ('good') applied to Ahithophel's counsel is the narrator's own evaluation, not a character's opinion. The narrator, speaking with omniscient authority, declares that the rejected plan was genuinely superior. This prevents the reader from dismissing Ahithophel as incompetent and forces the theological conclusion: the outcome was not determined by the quality of the advice but by the will of the LORD.

key_terms.semantic_range: counsel, advice, strategic plan, deliberative wisdom, purpose, design
key_terms.note: In this verse etsah appears three times — Hushai's etsah, Ahithophel's etsah, and Ahithophel's etsah again qualified as 'good.' The repetition turns the word into a battlefield: two counsels clash, and the LORD adjudicates. The narrator's admission that the defeated counsel was 'good' (tovah) makes this a theological statement about divine sovereignty overriding human wisdom.

key_terms.semantic_range: to break, to frustrate, to annul, to make void, to violate (a covenant or plan)
key_terms.note: The verb parar in the Hiphil means to cause something to fail, to shatter a plan or agreement. It is covenant-breaking language applied to political counsel. The LORD treats Ahithophel's plan as something that must be actively broken — not ignored or overlooked but deliberately demolished. The same verb is used when God 'breaks' the counsel of the nations in Psalm 33:10.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר חוּשַׁ֗י אֶל־צָד֤וֹק וְאֶל־אֶבְיָתָר֙ הַכֹּ֣הֲנִ֔ים כָּזֹ֣את וְכָזֹ֗את יָעַ֤ץ אֲחִיתֹ֙פֶל֙ אֶת־אַבְשָׁל֔וֹם וְאֵ֖ת זִקְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל וְכָזֹ֥את וְכָזֹ֖את יָעַ֥צְתִּי אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Then said Hushai unto Zadok and to Abiathar the priests, Thus and thus did Ahithophel counsel Absalom and the elders of Israel; and thus and thus have I counselled.
verses.rendering: Hushai then told the priests Zadok and Abiathar, "Ahithophel advised Absalom and the elders of Israel to do such-and-such, but I advised such-and-such.
translator_notes: The seamless transition from the council chamber to the spy network demonstrates the narrator's skill in weaving political drama with espionage thriller. Hushai does not pause to celebrate his rhetorical victory; he immediately ensures that David receives the intelligence needed to survive regardless of which plan Absalom ultimately executes.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעַתָּ֗ה שִׁלְח֤וּ מְהֵרָה֙ וְהַגִּ֣ידוּ לְדָוִ֣ד לֵאמֹ֔ר אַל־תָּ֣לֶן הַלַּ֔יְלָה בְּעַרְב֖וֹת הַמִּדְבָּ֑ר וְגַם֙ עָב֣וֹר תַּעֲבֹ֔ר פֶּן־יְבֻלַּ֥ע לַמֶּ֖לֶךְ וּלְכָל־הָעָ֥ם אֲשֶׁר־אִתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Now therefore send quickly, and tell David, saying, Lodge not this night in the plains of the wilderness, but speedily pass over; lest the king be swallowed up, and all the people that are with him.
verses.rendering: Now send word to David at once and tell him, 'Do not spend tonight at the fords of the wilderness. Cross over immediately, or the king and all the people with him will be swallowed up.'"
verses.expanded_rendering: Hushai's urgency is palpable: shilchu meherah ('send quickly'). Despite having successfully sabotaged Ahithophel's plan, Hushai does not trust the outcome — Absalom might change his mind, or Ahithophel might persuade him privately. The instruction to David is specific: al talen hallaylah be-arvot hammidbar ('do not lodge tonight in the desert plains'). The arvot are the flat areas near the Jordan where a fleeing army might camp — exposed terrain, vulnerable to pursuit. The command avvor ta'avor ('crossing, you must cross') uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis: get across the Jordan without delay. The reason — pen yevulla lammelekh ('lest the king be swallowed up') — uses the verb bala ('to swallow'), a word for total consumption. David's company could be annihilated in the open wilderness if Absalom acts faster than expected.
translator_notes: The verb bala ('to swallow') is used for catastrophic, total destruction — Korah's company was 'swallowed' by the earth (Numbers 16:32). Hushai's fear is not partial defeat but complete annihilation. The urgency of his message suggests that even after his rhetorical success, the situation remains precarious — Absalom's decision could be reversed at any moment.

verses.text_hebrew: וִיהוֹנָתָ֨ן וַאֲחִימַ֜עַץ עֹמְדִ֣ים בְּעֵין־רֹגֵ֗ל וְהָלְכָ֤ה הַשִּׁפְחָה֙ וְהִגִּ֣ידָה לָהֶ֔ם וְהֵ֣ם יֵלְכ֔וּ וְהִגִּ֖ידוּ לַמֶּ֣לֶךְ דָּוִ֑ד כִּ֣י לֹ֤א יוּכְלוּ֙ לְהֵ֣רָא֔וֹת לָב֖וֹא הָעִֽירָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz stayed by Enrogel; for they might not be seen to come into the city: and a wench went and told them; and they went and told king David.
verses.rendering: Jonathan and Ahimaaz were stationed at En-rogel, since they could not risk being seen entering the city. A female servant would go out and relay the information to them, and they would carry the message to King David.
verses.expanded_rendering: The narrator now reveals the mechanics of David's spy network. Jonathan (son of Abiathar) and Ahimaaz (son of Zadok) — both priests' sons — are positioned at En-rogel ('the spring of the fuller'), a water source just south of Jerusalem in the Kidron Valley. Their location is strategic: close enough to receive messages from the city but outside the walls where they would be recognized. The communication chain is elegant: Hushai tells the priests, a female servant (ha-shiphchah) carries the message outside the city to the two young men, and they run it to David. The servant girl is the critical link — a woman going to a spring would attract no suspicion, making her the perfect courier. The note ki lo yukhelu lehera'ot lavo ha-irah ('because they could not risk being seen coming into the city') explains why this relay system is necessary: Jonathan and Ahimaaz are known associates of David, and entering Jerusalem would expose the entire network.
translator_notes: En-rogel is identified with modern Bir Ayyub ('Job's Well'), at the junction of the Kidron and Hinnom valleys. It was a public water source where women regularly came to wash clothes or draw water — the perfect cover for passing intelligence. The word shiphchah ('female servant, maidservant') indicates a person of low social visibility, exactly the kind of operative who can move through a city without drawing attention. The narrator's appreciation for tradecraft is evident.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֤רְא אֹתָם֙ נַ֔עַר וַיַּגֵּ֖ד לְאַבְשָׁלֹ֑ם וַיֵּלְכ֤וּ שְׁנֵיהֶם֙ מְהֵרָ֔ה וַיָּבֹ֗אוּ אֶל־בֵּ֥ית אִישׁ֙ בְּבַ֣חוּרִ֔ים וְל֥וֹ בְאֵ֖ר בֶּחָצֵ֑ר וַיֵּרְד֖וּ שָֽׁם׃
verses.text_kjv: Nevertheless a lad saw them, and told Absalom: but they went both of them away quickly, and came to a man's house in Bahurim, which had a well in his court; and they went down thither.
verses.rendering: But a young man spotted them and reported it to Absalom. So the two of them hurried away and came to the house of a man in Bahurim who had a well in his courtyard, and they climbed down into it.
verses.expanded_rendering: The spy operation nearly collapses: vayyar otam na'ar ('a young man saw them'). The same word na'ar that described the Amalekite messenger in chapter 1 here describes a chance observer who recognizes the two priests' sons and reports to Absalom. The intelligence chain is compromised. Jonathan and Ahimaaz flee meherah ('quickly') to Bahurim — the same village where Shimei cursed David in 2 Samuel 16:5. What was hostile territory in the previous chapter now provides a safe house. The detail velo ve'er bechatser ('and he had a well in his courtyard') is specific and practical: a dry or low-water well in a private courtyard is the ideal hiding place, invisible from the street and accessible only to the homeowner. The two men descend into it — vayyerdu sham ('they went down there') — disappearing from the surface.
translator_notes: Bahurim was a Benjaminite village on the road from Jerusalem to the Jordan. Its reappearance here creates a narrative echo: the village that produced David's curser (Shimei) also produces David's protector (the unnamed householder). The well (be'er) as a hiding place is a recurring motif — Joseph was thrown into a bor (pit/cistern) in Genesis 37:24, and Jeremiah was lowered into one in Jeremiah 38:6. Here the well saves rather than endangers.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתִּקַּ֣ח הָאִשָּׁ֗ה וַתִּפְרֹ֤שׂ אֶת־הַמָּסָךְ֙ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י הַבְּאֵ֔ר וַתִּשְׁטַ֥ח עָלָ֖יו הָרִפ֑וֹת וְלֹ֥א נוֹדַ֖ע דָּבָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the woman took and spread a covering over the well's mouth, and spread ground corn thereon; and the thing was not known.
verses.rendering: The woman of the house took a covering and spread it over the mouth of the well, then scattered grain on top of it. Nothing was noticed.
verses.expanded_rendering: The unnamed woman acts with quick ingenuity: she takes a masakh ('covering, cloth screen') and spreads it over the well's opening, then scatters riphot ('crushed grain, groats') on top. The grain drying on a cloth in a courtyard would be a perfectly ordinary domestic sight — no one would think to look beneath it. The final clause velo noda davar ('and nothing was known,' meaning 'the matter was not discovered') confirms the success of the deception. This woman joins a tradition of resourceful women in the Hebrew Bible who protect fugitives through cunning: Rahab hiding the spies under flax on her roof (Joshua 2:6), Michal disguising a pillow as David in his bed (1 Samuel 19:13). The narrator offers no name, no backstory, no motive — only the decisive action that saves the intelligence mission.
translator_notes: The word riphot is a hapax legomenon in some analyses (or nearly so), referring to crushed or split grain spread out to dry. The domestic ordinariness of the camouflage is the point: grain drying on a cloth over a flat surface is so unremarkable that it renders the well invisible. The woman's quick thinking parallels Rahab's in Joshua 2:6, where flax stalks laid out on a roof served the same concealing function.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּבֹ֜אוּ עַבְדֵ֤י אַבְשָׁלוֹם֙ אֶל־הָ֣אִשָּׁ֔ה הַבַּ֖יְתָה וַיֹּאמְר֗וּ אַיֵּ֤ה אֲחִימַ֙עַץ֙ וִיה֣וֹנָתָ֔ן וַתֹּ֤אמֶר לָהֶם֙ הָֽאִשָּׁ֔ה עָבְר֖וּ מִיכַ֣ל הַמָּ֑יִם וַיְבַקְשׁ֥וּ וְלֹ֣א מָצָ֔אוּ וַיָּשֻׁ֖בוּ יְרוּשָׁלָֽ͏ִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And when Absalom's servants came to the woman to the house, they said, Where is Ahimaaz and Jonathan? And the woman said unto them, They be gone over the brook of water. And when they had sought and could not find them, they returned to Jerusalem.
verses.rendering: When Absalom's men arrived at the house and asked the woman, "Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?" she told them, "They crossed over the stream." The men searched but found nothing, and returned to Jerusalem.
verses.expanded_rendering: Absalom's servants arrive with a direct question: ayyeh Achima'ats vi-Yhonatan ('where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?'). The woman answers with calm misdirection: averu mikhal hammayim ('they crossed over the water channel'). The word mikhal is rare — it may mean a small watercourse, a channel, or a brook. She sends the pursuers in the wrong direction, away from the well in her courtyard and toward a body of water that would plausibly be crossed by men heading toward the Jordan and David's camp. The search fails — vayvaqeshu velo matsa'u ('they searched and did not find') — and the pursuers give up and return to Jerusalem. The economy of the narration mirrors the efficiency of the deception: three clauses cover the pursuit, the failure, and the retreat. The woman's lie, like Rahab's in Joshua 2:4-5, is presented without moral commentary. The narrator is interested in results, not ethics.
translator_notes: The word mikhal (מִיכַל) is textually uncertain — some manuscripts read mikhal as a proper noun or place name, others as a common noun meaning 'channel' or 'brook.' The Septuagint reads 'they passed on a little way past the water.' We render it as 'stream' to capture the sense of a small watercourse that could plausibly be crossed by men in flight.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֣י אַחֲרֵי֮ לֶכְתָּם֒ וַיַּעֲל֣וּ מֵהַבְּאֵ֔ר וַיֵּ֣לְכ֔וּ וַיַּגִּ֖דוּ לַמֶּ֣לֶךְ דָּוִ֑ד וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ אֶל־דָּוִ֗ד ק֣וּמוּ וְעִבְר֤וּ מְהֵרָה֙ אֶת־הַמַּ֔יִם כִּי־כָ֛כָה יָעַ֥ץ עֲלֵיכֶ֖ם אֲחִיתֹֽפֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, after they were departed, that they came up out of the well, and went and told king David, and said unto David, Arise, and pass quickly over the water: for thus hath Ahithophel counselled against you.
verses.rendering: After the pursuers had gone, the two men climbed out of the well and went to King David. They told him, "Get moving — cross the water immediately, because Ahithophel has counseled such-and-such against you."
verses.expanded_rendering: The timing is precise: acharei lekhtam ('after their departure') — only when the coast is clear do Jonathan and Ahimaaz emerge. They waste no time: vayyedu vayyagidu lammelekh David ('they went and they reported to King David'). The message is urgent and actionable: qumu ve-ivru meherah et hammayim ('rise and cross the water quickly'). They relay the critical intelligence: ki khakhah ya'ats alekhem Achitophel ('because thus Ahithophel has counseled against you'). Note that they report Ahithophel's plan, not Hushai's — David needs to know the worst-case scenario. Even though Hushai's plan was chosen, the messengers cannot guarantee that Absalom will not revert to Ahithophel's faster, deadlier option. David must act on the assumption that the night strike is still possible.
translator_notes: The phrase ya'ats alekhem ('he counseled against you') uses the preposition al in its adversarial sense — Ahithophel's counsel was directed against David, not merely about David. The messengers convey both the content of the threat and its hostile intent.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֣קָם דָּוִ֗ד וְכָל־הָעָם֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתּ֔וֹ וַיַּעַבְר֖וּ אֶת־הַיַּרְדֵּ֑ן עַד־א֣וֹר הַבֹּ֔קֶר עַד־אֶחָ֣ד לֹ֥א נֶעְדָּ֛ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר לֹא־עָבַ֖ר אֶת־הַיַּרְדֵּֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Then David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan: by the morning light there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan.
verses.rendering: David set out with all the people who were with him, and they crossed the Jordan. By the time morning light broke, not a single one was left who had not crossed the Jordan.
translator_notes: The phrase ad or habboqer ('until morning light') indicates an all-night march and river crossing — an extraordinary feat of endurance for an exhausted and mixed company that included non-combatants. The narrator's emphasis that 'not one was missing' is both military language (a full muster count after a tactical movement) and theological reassurance: David's people are intact.

The Jordan crossing in reverse — east instead of west — inverts the Exodus/Conquest pattern. David is not entering the promised land but leaving it. Yet the narrator presents the crossing as deliverance, not exile. The Jordan functions as protection, not as a boundary of promise. This geographic reversal adds depth to the chapter's theme of God working through unexpected means.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲחִיתֹ֣פֶל רָאָ֗ה כִּ֣י לֹ֤א נֶעֶשְׂתָה֙ עֲצָת֔וֹ וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ֙ אֶת־הַחֲמ֔וֹר וַיָּ֛קָם וַיֵּ֥לֶךְ אֶל־בֵּית֖וֹ אֶל־עִיר֑וֹ וַיְצַ֤ו אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ֙ וַיֵּחָנַ֔ק וַיָּ֕מָת וַיִּקָּבֵ֖ר בְּקֶ֥בֶר אָבִֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: And when Ahithophel saw that his counsel was not followed, he saddled his ass, and arose, and gat him home to his house, to his city, and put his household in order, and hanged himself, and died, and was buried in the sepulchre of his father.
verses.rendering: When Ahithophel saw that his counsel had not been followed, he saddled his donkey, set out for home in his own town, set his household affairs in order, then hanged himself. He died and was buried in his father's tomb.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is one of the most remarkable passages in the Hebrew Bible for what it does not say. Ahithophel's suicide is narrated in six terse clauses, each one a single action: he saw (ra'ah) that his counsel was rejected, he saddled (vayyachbosh) his donkey, he rose and went (vayyaqam vayyelekh) to his house in his city, he set his affairs in order (vaytsav el beito), he hanged himself (vayyechanaq), and he died (vayyamot). A seventh clause adds: he was buried in his father's tomb (vayyiqqaver beqever aviv). There is no editorial commentary, no moral judgment, no theological explanation. The narrator offers neither condemnation nor sympathy. Ahithophel sees the strategic situation with perfect clarity: his counsel was rejected, the inferior plan will fail, Absalom will lose, and when David returns to power, Ahithophel — who defected from David's inner circle — will face execution as a traitor. He chooses death on his own terms. The phrase vaytsav el beito ('he commanded concerning his house' or 'he set his house in order') is the same language used for patriarchs preparing for death (2 Kings 20:1, Isaiah 38:1). Ahithophel dies not in panic but with the deliberation of a man settling his estate. The burial in his father's tomb indicates that his family honored him with an ancestral burial — there is no note of disgrace.
translator_notes: The verb chanaq ('to strangle, to hang') in the Niphal (vayyechanaq) indicates self-hanging. This is the only explicit suicide by hanging in the Hebrew Bible. The verb is rare — it appears in Job 7:15 ('my soul chooses strangling') and Nahum 2:12 (a lion strangling prey). The clinical brevity of the account — no setting, no last words, no witnesses — stands in sharp contrast to the elaborate death scenes of other biblical figures.

The phrase vaytsav el beito ('he set his house in order') uses tsavah, the same verb used when Hezekiah is told to 'set your house in order, for you are about to die' (2 Kings 20:1). Ahithophel is his own prophet: he diagnoses his own death and prepares for it with the same orderliness he brought to his political counsel. The narrator treats his suicide with the same matter-of-fact tone used for natural deaths, offering neither the condemnation that later tradition would apply nor the heroic framing that Greek literature might give a fallen counselor.

verses.text_hebrew: וְדָוִ֖ד בָּ֣א מַחֲנָ֑יְמָה וְאַבְשָׁלֹ֗ם עָבַר֙ אֶת־הַיַּרְדֵּ֔ן ה֖וּא וְכָל־אִ֥ישׁ יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל עִמּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Then David came to Mahanaim. And Absalom passed over Jordan, he and all the men of Israel with him.
verses.rendering: David arrived at Mahanaim, and Absalom crossed the Jordan — he and all the men of Israel with him.
translator_notes: Mahanaim served briefly as the capital of Saul's son Ish-bosheth (2 Samuel 2:8), making it a site of royal legitimacy in Transjordan. David's choice of Mahanaim is both tactical (a fortified city in defensible terrain) and political (a site associated with Israelite kingship). The parallel structure of this verse — David goes east, Absalom follows east — collapses the geographic distance between them and signals that confrontation is imminent.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת־עֲמָשָׂ֗א שָׂ֧ם אַבְשָׁל֛וֹם תַּ֥חַת יוֹאָ֖ב עַל־הַצָּבָ֑א וַעֲמָשָׂ֣א בֶן־אִ֗ישׁ וּשְׁמוֹ֙ יִתְרָ֣א הַיִּשְׂרְאֵלִ֔י אֲשֶׁר־בָּא֙ אֶל־אֲבִיגַ֣יִל בַּת־נָחָ֔שׁ אֲח֥וֹת צְרוּיָ֖ה אֵ֥ם יוֹאָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: And Absalom made Amasa captain of the host instead of Joab. Which Amasa was a man's son, whose name was Ithra an Israelite, that went in to Abigail the daughter of Nahash, sister to Zeruiah Joab's mother.
verses.rendering: Absalom had appointed Amasa over the army in place of Joab. Amasa was the son of a man named Ithra the Israelite, who had married Abigail daughter of Nahash, the sister of Zeruiah, Joab's mother.
translator_notes: The genealogical aside interrupts the narrative flow in a way typical of Hebrew historical writing — the narrator pauses the action to establish family relationships that explain political alignments. The identification of Ithra as 'the Israelite' (ha-Yisre'eli) is odd if he is indeed an Israelite (1 Chronicles 2:17 calls him 'Jether the Ishmaelite,' which may be the original reading). The textual variants suggest early scribal confusion about Ithra's ethnic identity.

Amasa's appointment will have lasting consequences: after the rebellion fails, David will offer Amasa Joab's position as a reconciliation gesture (2 Samuel 19:13), and Joab will murder Amasa for it (2 Samuel 20:10). The seed planted in this verse grows into one of the bloodiest episodes in David's reign.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּ֤חַן יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ וְאַבְשָׁל֔וֹם אֶ֖רֶץ הַגִּלְעָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: So Israel and Absalom pitched in the land of Gilead.
verses.rendering: Israel and Absalom made camp in the land of Gilead.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verse is strikingly brief: vayyichan Yisra'el ve-Avshalom erets haGil'ad ('and Israel and Absalom camped in the land of Gilead'). The phrase 'Israel and Absalom' is telling — the narrator names the nation and the rebel prince as co-belligerents, implying that Absalom has successfully claimed the identity of 'Israel' for his faction. Gilead, the Transjordanian territory, becomes the theater of the coming civil war. Both armies are now east of the Jordan, in the same general region, and the proximity signals that battle is near. The brevity of the verse creates a pause between the espionage narrative and the hospitality scene that follows — a moment of stillness before the storm.
translator_notes: The coupling of 'Israel and Absalom' as a single unit implicitly denies David the title of Israel's leader. Throughout this rebellion narrative, the narrator carefully tracks which side gets to claim the name 'Israel' — here it belongs to Absalom. David's side is simply 'the people with him.' The political claim embedded in the naming is deliberate.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֕י כְּב֥וֹא דָוִ֖ד מַחֲנָ֑יְמָה וְשֹׁבִ֨י בֶן־נָחָ֜שׁ מֵרַבַּ֣ת בְּנֵי־עַמּ֗וֹן וּמָכִ֤יר בֶּן־עַמִּיאֵל֙ מִלֹּ֣א דְבָ֔ר וּבַרְזִלַּ֥י הַגִּלְעָדִ֖י מֵרֹגְלִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, when David was come to Mahanaim, that Shobi the son of Nahash of Rabbah of the children of Ammon, and Machir the son of Ammiel of Lodebar, and Barzillai the Gileadite of Rogelim,
verses.rendering: When David arrived at Mahanaim, Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, and Makir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim
translator_notes: The three-name list follows a geographic pattern: Rabbah (Ammon, southeast), Lo-debar (northern Gilead), and Rogelim (central Gilead). David's support comes from across the Transjordanian region, not from a single locale. This geographic spread suggests that David's alliances east of the Jordan were deep and well-maintained.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenantal kindness, steadfast faithfulness, mercy, devotion, committed generosity
key_terms.note: Although the word chesed does not appear explicitly in verses 27-29, the entire scene is a demonstration of chesed in action. These three men bring provisions to a fugitive king at personal risk — Absalom controls 'all Israel,' and supporting David is an act of political defiance. Chesed in the Hebrew Bible is not sentiment but costly action: it is loyalty made tangible through sacrifice, provision, and presence. Shobi's chesed echoes his father Nahash's chesed toward David (2 Samuel 10:2). Makir's chesed echoes his earlier sheltering of Mephibosheth. Barzillai's chesed will become proverbial — David will remember it on his deathbed. The chapter that began with political counsel ends with covenantal kindness, and the narrator's juxtaposition implies that chesed, not etsah, is what ultimately sustains David.

verses.text_hebrew: מִשְׁכָּ֤ב וְסַפּוֹת֙ וּכְלִ֣י יוֹצֵ֔ר וְחִטִּ֥ים וּשְׂעֹרִ֖ים וְקֶ֣מַח וְקָלִ֑י וּפ֥וֹל וַעֲדָשִׁ֖ים וְקָלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Brought beds, and basons, and earthen vessels, and wheat, and barley, and flour, and parched corn, and beans, and lentiles, and parched pulse,
verses.rendering: brought bedding, basins, pottery, wheat, barley, flour, roasted grain, beans, lentils, parched seeds,
verses.expanded_rendering: The provision list is remarkably detailed, moving from household goods to staple foods. Mishkav ('bedding, sleeping mats') addresses the most immediate physical need of exhausted refugees. Sappot ('basins, bowls') and keli yotser ('potter's vessels, earthenware') provide the basic equipment for eating and drinking — David's company fled without domestic supplies. Then the food catalogue begins: chittim ('wheat'), se'orim ('barley'), qemach ('flour'), qali ('roasted grain'), pol ('beans'), adashim ('lentils'), and qali again (possibly 'parched seeds' or a variant item in the same category). This is not luxury food but sustenance: the calorie-dense staples that would feed a large group of hungry people. The list's very ordinariness is its power — these allies are not offering symbolic gifts but practical survival supplies. They have assessed what a refugee army needs and delivered it.
translator_notes: The repetition of qali ('roasted/parched grain') has puzzled commentators. Some emend the second occurrence to qela'ot ('parched grain of a different type'); others accept the repetition as emphasis. The Septuagint and Vulgate handle the list slightly differently. We retain the Masoretic text's repetition. The list as a whole represents the full range of a Transjordanian agricultural economy: grain crops, legumes, and prepared foods.

verses.text_hebrew: וּדְבַ֣שׁ וְחֶמְאָ֗ה וְצֹ֤אן וּשְׁפ֥וֹת בָּקָ֛ר הִגִּ֥ישׁוּ לְדָוִ֖ד וְלָעָ֣ם אֲשֶׁר־אִתּ֑וֹ לֶאֱכ֕וֹל כִּ֣י אָמְר֔וּ הָעָ֗ם רָעֵ֛ב וְעָיֵ֥ף וְצָמֵ֖א בַּמִּדְבָּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And honey, and butter, and sheep, and cheese of kine, for David, and for the people that were with him, to eat: for they said, The people is hungry, and weary, and thirsty, in the wilderness.
verses.rendering: honey, curds, sheep, and cheese from the herd. They brought all of this to David and the people with him to eat, for they said, "The people are hungry, exhausted, and thirsty from the wilderness."
verses.expanded_rendering: The provision list continues with higher-value items: devash ('honey'), chem'ah ('curds, butter'), tson ('sheep' — live animals for meat), and shephot baqar ('cheese of cattle,' probably a pressed dairy product). The verb higgishu ('they brought near, they presented') indicates a formal delivery, not a casual gift. Then the narrator gives the donors' reasoning in their own words: ki ameru ha-am ra'ev ve-ayeph ve-tsame bamidbar ('because they said: the people are hungry, and weary, and thirsty in the wilderness'). The three-fold description — hungry, exhausted, thirsty — captures the total physical depletion of David's company. The word midbar ('wilderness') echoes Israel's wilderness wandering after the Exodus: David's people are again in the desert, again dependent on provision that comes from outside themselves. The chapter ends not with military strategy or political intrigue but with a meal. After twenty-nine verses of counsel, espionage, pursuit, suicide, and forced marches, the final image is of people being fed. The practical chesed of Shobi, Makir, and Barzillai sustains David when all the brilliant counsel in the world could not.
translator_notes: The phrase shephot baqar is difficult — shephot may derive from shaphat ('to judge') or from an otherwise unknown root meaning 'cheese' or 'milk products.' Most translators follow the Septuagint and Vulgate in reading it as a dairy product, likely a pressed or aged cheese. We render it as 'cheese from the herd' following the majority interpretation.

The donors' quoted speech — 'the people are hungry, exhausted, and thirsty in the wilderness' — is the chapter's final word on David's condition. It corrects Hushai's terrifying portrait of David as a raging bear: in reality, David's people are starving refugees in need of bread, water, and a place to sleep. The gap between Hushai's propaganda and the donors' compassion captures the chapter's dual nature: it is simultaneously a story of political manipulation and a story of human kindness.
